approach: the issue names three divergences and argues they are ONE pass rather than three, and source bears that out: a single fail() helper in cli.ts with 22 call sites carries the stream, the code and the structure together, so moving an error to stdout, giving it a stable code and giving it the right status are the same edit to the same line. Splitting them would touch every command three times

the upstream AXI spec was read directly rather than this repo's restatement of it, and that is the whole method of this CR: the restatement is the defect under repair, and it has already misled two missions — #31 read the dropped exit 2 as the code's non-existence and invented a meaning for it, and #37 corrected that. Trusting the local paragraph a third time was the one failure mode guaranteed to recur

reading upstream surfaced a FOURTH divergence the issue does not name, and it is the same inversion wearing a different hat: AXI puts data, errors AND suggestions on stdout, rendering suggestions as a help[N] block inside the payload, while this node's #9 sends next-step lines to stderr. The node's #9 diverges twice over, because AXI also says to OMIT a suggestion when the output is self-contained, where the node demands one from every command. Folded in on the requester's call — same helper, same commands, same stream decision

the requester settled the two questions the issue said needed deciding first, and both go the conformant way: follow AXI on streams here and FILE the cyberplace inversion rather than fix it in this CR, so the org's shared node is not rewritten by a bin that does not own it; and RATIFY the re-open of the frozen bare-send scenario, which pins both halves wrong (written to stderr, exits 1) and so is narrowed on two counts, not one

Clearance fires and is ratified. Compatibility does NOT fire and this was checked rather than assumed: the package is 0.0.0 with nothing shipped, so a breaking change to the exit-code and stream contract exceeds no ceiling. A changeset is still owed

title: cyberplace's axi node inverts AXI on streams, word for word
body: The shared axi node in cyberplace states that errors go to stderr and that stderr carries the agent-facing affordances. AXI states the opposite: stdout carries data, errors and suggestions so the agent can read and act on them, and stderr is debug the agent does not read. cyber-mux's node stated the same inversion and this CR corrected it there, but the sibling node is not this bin's to rewrite. Until it lands, an agent moving between the two bins meets errors on different streams. Discovered while correcting this repo's own node against the upstream spec.

title: exists spends exit 1 on gone, which diverges from AXI's 1 = error
body: exists is a predicate: it answers live at 0, gone at 1, ambiguous at 2. Its 1 is an answer rather than an error, which is the framing grep, POSIX test and systemctl is-active take, and it is deliberate. But AXI reserves 1 for an error, so this genuinely diverges from the code set the contract node restates in full. This CR corrected the label only: the corpus had called it an amendment to a 0/1 set, which was wrong twice over, since the set was always three codes and what diverges is the meaning of 1. Whether to keep the divergence, or move gone to 0 as a definitive empty state, is unsettled.

title: Should the content-first principle extend from the bare binary to a command group?
body: AXI scopes content-first to the bare binary run with no arguments. This repo's node had restated it as covering any command group invoked with no subcommand, which is a widening the source does not support and which was never argued as an extension. This CR retracts the widening and the reasoning built on it. The extension may well be right for a data-bearing group, whose bare form arguably owes its live view for exactly the reason the principle gives. That question belongs to the contract rather than to any one adopter, and nothing is asserted here about the others.

title: cyber-mux has no home view, so two AXI principles are unbuilt together
body: Running the bare binary prints help rather than live content, which the content-first principle rejects, and the consistent-help principle separately requires that same no-argument view to identify the tool by its executable path with the home directory collapsed, plus a one-sentence description, before any data. The node had dropped that identity requirement entirely, an omission of the same class as the dropped third exit code that this CR's lineage began with. Both gaps are one missing surface and should land together.

title: A truncation hint and a placement suggestion sit on stderr, contradicting the contextual-disclosure correction this CR just made
body: This CR corrected the contextual-disclosure principle to put suggestions on stdout in the payload and to omit them when the output is self-contained. Two shipped paths contradict the corrected text and were left untouched because the layout suite is frozen and its re-open was not cleared. One writes a note to stderr that a workspace holds more tabs than were captured, which is the reveal-a-truncated-list case the principle names verbatim and puts on stdout. The other writes an actionable suggestion to stderr telling the caller which flag would have grouped what it opened. It names a completion claim this CR makes: the node reports the principle as unimplemented, which is false while those two paths ship. Needs a cleared re-open of the layout suite.

approach: Clearance is EXTENDED a second time, ratified in-session: from the six mux.feature scenarios the stream change touched to the layout.feature suite as well. The spec-judge's round 2 found a genuine Conflict, not a mere gap: one validator (isValidLayoutName) is contracted at exit 1 by frozen layout.feature and at exit 2 by this CR's new row, through the same function, so no implementation can satisfy both. The narrower reading was available and declined — the stream and structure halves conflict with NOTHING in layout (no layout error is pinned to any stream; its stderr pins are all warnings), so streams+structure could have landed on layout while its exit codes waited. The requester chose full conformance in one pass instead: audit every exit-1 pin in layout.feature and reclassify each as a genuine failure that stays 1 or a usage error that becomes 2. This keeps the CR's one-pass thesis literally true across all ~15 verbs rather than carving layout out of it

why: Draft -> Approved. Three cold spec-judge rounds, R1 and R2 failed all three lenses and R3 passed clean (all lenses PASS, ALIGNED true, no blocker, no open marker; check-suite and check-spec-state green, both features parse). Clearance fired twice and both re-opens were ratified in-session: the six mux.feature stream scenarios up front, and layout.feature after R2 surfaced the isValidLayoutName Conflict. Compatibility does not fire (package 0.0.0). Both suites stay @frozen at the new content; no production code changed. Deliver builds the fail() sweep across all ~15 verbs against this frozen suite.

Approved -> Implemented. Cold sdd-impl-judge re-derived every frozen scenario's oracle from source, drove the real binary for the load-bearing cases, and re-ran the full turbo verify itself: 7/7 tasks, 559 tests, biome ci clean, both features parse. IMPLEMENTATION_PASS true, no blocker. The 22 fail() sites became one CliError model on stdout with distinct codes and help, usage errors at exit 2, backend text translated not forwarded; the partial-apply result-payload invariant kept out of the error path. The four layout usage errors moved to 2 and the other 19 exit-1 pins did not regress. Seam and adapters byte-identical to HEAD. Self-asserted within the auto-spec leash; this CR's spec gate was ratified in-session by the requester.
